name: Right View
body:
Right view means understanding the Four Noble Truths, the law of kamma (that intentional actions have consequences), and dependent origination (that nothing exists in isolation, everything arises in dependence on conditions). It also means recognizing what is wholesome and what is unwholesome — not as moral rules imposed from outside, but as the actual structure of how the mind generates suffering or freedom.

Right view is listed first because it is the orientation of the whole path. Without it, the other seven branches become rituals or rules. With it, they become a coherent training. The Buddha said in MN 117 that right view is the forerunner — like the first light before dawn.

In practice, right view is not a belief you adopt. It is something you verify in your own experience, slowly, by watching the mind do what the suttas describe.

name: Right Intention
short: The orientation of the heart before action.
body:
Right intention has three components: the intention of renunciation (letting go of grasping), the intention of goodwill (mettā), and the intention of harmlessness (karuṇā — not wanting to cause harm).

This is the bridge between view and action. Right view sees clearly; right intention forms the heart's response to what is seen. If right view sees that craving causes suffering, right intention chooses to incline away from craving. If right view sees that all beings want to be free of pain, right intention chooses goodwill rather than hostility.

Intention is not the same as motivation in the modern sense. It is what the heart actually leans toward, beneath the surface of what we tell ourselves. The Buddha was clear in AN 6.63 that intention IS kamma — the seed from which the whole tree of action grows.

name: Right Speech
body:
Right speech means abstaining from four kinds of wrong speech: false speech, divisive speech, harsh speech, and idle chatter. The positive form is speech that is true, that brings beings together rather than dividing them, that is gentle rather than cutting, and that is meaningful rather than just filling silence.

The Buddha gave a specific test in AN 5.198 for whether speech is wholesome: it should be spoken at the right time, be true, be gentle, be beneficial, and be spoken with a mind of goodwill. All five — not just truth alone. A true statement spoken at the wrong time with cutting intent is not right speech. A true statement spoken at the right time with care is.

Right speech is also the most underestimated branch of the path for modern practitioners. Most people will sit hours of meditation but say twenty thoughtless things between sits. The path is one whole — the cushion is not the only place it lives.

name: Right Action
body:
Right action means abstaining from killing, from taking what is not given, and from sexual misconduct. The traditional formulation focuses on these three because they are the bodily actions most likely to cause harm to oneself and others, and because their absence creates the conditions for a settled mind that can practice.

The deeper meaning is that right action is action that does not generate the kind of remorse and agitation that prevents the mind from gathering. The Buddha taught in AN 11.1 that ethical conduct is the proximate cause of non-remorse, which is the proximate cause of joy, which is the proximate cause of concentration. The path is sequential here, even though the branches are developed together.

A sit on top of an unethical day is much harder than a sit on top of an ethical day. This is not a moral judgment — it is a description of how the mind actually works.

name: Right Livelihood
short: Earning your life without harming others.
body:
Right livelihood means earning one's living in ways that do not cause harm. The Buddha named five kinds of trade lay disciples should avoid (AN 5.177): trade in weapons, in beings, in flesh, in intoxicants, and in poisons. The principle behind the list is broader than the list itself — any work whose primary effect is to cause suffering to others, or to encourage actions the path is meant to abandon, is incompatible with the path.

For most modern practitioners this is the hardest branch to think about clearly, because so much modern work is entangled with industries and effects we cannot fully see. The Buddha did not ask for moral purity. He asked for honest looking. What is your work actually doing in the world? Where is the harm? Where is the benefit? Can you live with the answers?

Right livelihood is also the place where the path is most public — where your practice meets your bills, your community, your commitments. It is harder than right speech and right action precisely because you cannot opt out of having a livelihood.

name: Right Effort
short: The fourfold effort, balanced like the lute strings.
body:
Right effort has four parts (AN 4.13, AN 4.14): the effort to prevent unwholesome states that have not yet arisen, the effort to abandon unwholesome states that have arisen, the effort to develop wholesome states that have not yet arisen, and the effort to maintain wholesome states that are present.

The crucial word is "right." Effort that is too tight produces strain, frustration, and burnout — the lute strings tightened past the breaking point (AN 6.55, the Sona Sutta). Effort that is too loose produces sloth and drift. Right effort is the middle tuning, where the mind is engaged enough to do the work but not so tight that it breaks itself in the doing.

Most practitioners err in one direction or the other. The serious ones tighten too much. The casual ones loosen too much. Notice which way you go, and adjust toward the other.

name: Right Mindfulness
short: The four foundations — body, feeling, mind, dhammas.
body:
Right mindfulness is the four foundations of mindfulness laid out in MN 10 (the Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta) and MN 119: contemplation of the body, of feeling tone (vedanā — pleasant, unpleasant, neutral), of mind, and of dhammas (mental phenomena, including the hindrances, the aggregates, the sense bases, the awakening factors, and the four noble truths themselves).

This is the practice you are training when you sit. Knowing the body as the body — not as "mine," not as a problem to fix. Knowing feeling tone as feeling tone — the bare pleasantness or unpleasantness of an experience, before the mind adds the story. Knowing mind states as mind states — "this is anger," "this is contentment" — without becoming them. Knowing the dhammas as the dhammas — the patterns the Buddha pointed to, recognized in your own direct experience.

MN 10 is the single most important text for daily practice. If you read only one sutta this year, read this one.

name: Right Concentration
short: The gathered mind that wisdom uses.
body:
Right concentration is traditionally defined as the four jhānas — the progressively refined states of meditative absorption described in MN 36, MN 39, and many other suttas. It is the gathered, unified, pliable mind that wisdom uses to do its real work.

Some modern teachers and lineages emphasize the formal jhānas; others emphasize "momentary concentration" or access concentration as sufficient for insight. The disagreement is real and ancient. What is not disputed is that some degree of concentration is necessary — a mind that is constantly scattered cannot see its own nature, no matter how good its intentions.

Right concentration is the eighth and last branch of the path because the others build it. Sīla (right speech, action, livelihood) creates non-remorse. Right effort and mindfulness create attention. Out of attention without remorse, concentration arises naturally. You cannot bypass the earlier branches and try to build concentration first — every serious tradition agrees on this.

If your concentration practice is not deepening, look earlier in the path for what is missing.
